quickInsight: When the Israelites said 'All that the Lord hath said will we do,' they weren't just agreeing to rules — they were choosing whose team they were on, and that same choice is in front of you every single day.
keyThemes: Making and keeping covenants — saying 'I'm in' and meaning it
Taking responsibility for how your choices affect others
Standing up for people who can't stand up for themselves
God's laws aren't restrictions — they're the playbook for a life that actually works
Seeing God's presence in your life when you keep your commitments
discussionQuestions: In Exodus 24:7, the Israelites said 'All that the Lord hath said will we do, and be obedient.' What's the difference between saying you'll do something and actually committing to it — like, what does real commitment look like at school or on a team?

These chapters have a lot of laws about making things right when you've wronged someone (restitution). Why do you think God cares so much about us fixing things when we mess up, instead of just saying sorry and moving on?

Exodus 22:21–27 talks about protecting strangers, widows, and orphans — basically anyone who's vulnerable. Who are the 'vulnerable' people in your world (at school, online, in your friend group), and what does it look like to have their back?

God told Moses He'd send blessings and protection if Israel kept the covenant (Exodus 23:20–26). Have you ever experienced blessings from keeping a commitment — even when it was hard or nobody was watching?
The Israelites had just come out of slavery and God immediately gave them a code for how to treat each other fairly. Why do you think that was one of the first things He addressed?
teachingIdeas: Start class by asking everyone to make a quick promise out loud — something small like 'I promise I'll hold this pen for the whole lesson.' See who keeps it. Then read Exodus 24:3,7 and talk about what it means to say 'I will do' versus actually doing. Covenants aren't just words — they're who you become.

Use a sports analogy: a coach's playbook isn't there to ruin your fun — it's how you win. Walk through a few of the laws in Exodus 21–23 and ask the guys to figure out the principle behind each one. Help them see that God's 'rules' are really His strategy guide for a life that works.

Tell a story (personal or from someone you know) about a time you had to make something right with someone — not just apologize, but actually fix it. Connect it to the restitution laws in Exodus 22. Real repentance has action behind it.

Show Exodus 24:9–11 where Moses, Aaron, and the elders actually see God and eat a meal in His presence. Ask: what would it be like to sit down and eat with God? Then connect it to the sacrament — every Sunday, they're invited to that same kind of sacred, covenant meal.
activities: Covenant Challenge Week: Each young man picks one specific commitment to keep perfectly for 7 days — scripture study, no gossip, complimenting someone daily, phone off by 10pm — whatever they choose. At the next activity, share how it went. The point isn't perfection, it's learning what it feels like to be 'all in' on something.

Service Blitz for the Vulnerable: As a quorum, identify someone in the ward or neighborhood who could use help — an elderly member, a single parent, a new family. Plan and execute a service project during mutual. Connect it back to Exodus 22:21–27 and God's charge to look out for people who need it.

Restitution Role-Play: Set up a few real-life scenarios (you broke a friend's trust, you said something hurtful online, you let a teammate down) and have the guys work through what real restitution looks like — not just 'sorry,' but what would you actually do to make it right? Make it practical and specific.
